speaker: interviewer
text: It's rolling bars, and we'll do the slate. OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Your kids are still in a [NON-ENGLISH] right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You too?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you working now?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm just doing this and that, you know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This and that? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's what you do when your kids are little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date today is Wednesday, April the 5th, 1995. The survivor is Malka Hahn. Her interviewer will be Mindy Kornberg. And the interview will take place in Toronto, Canada. And the language of the interview is English. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please introduce yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Malka Hahn. My maiden name is Kartagena. I was born in Poland-- Rudnik-- the city of Rudnik, and spent my first two years there. After that, my parents moved to Czechoslovakia, to the Sudeten part of it. That was in 1929, where I spent--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason they moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was after the first world war. And they moved there to have a better life. It was mainly my mother who was motivated to get out of Poland, which she hated.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had been there many years-- the family had been there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family, yeah, was there for many years, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both sides of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both sides of the family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and so what city did you move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved to Reichenberg, in Czech Liberec. There was a small group of Jews who had come from Poland. Some of them were my parents' friends who settled there. And they encouraged them greatly to come and join them and live there, where things were a lot better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what was life like there as far as you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from your younger years?

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was a-- life was good. It was a pretty city with nice surroundings-- mountains and gardens. And this group of refugees were mostly busy in-- in the textile business, which was typical of that area. And my father was very reluctant to come, because he was afraid that Jewish life would not be strong enough, and was afraid to bring up children in that environment. But he did a lot to change that once they decided to move there. He was gonna forgo the textile business, which my mother had started, and-- and learned shechita in order to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's explain what that-- it's a ritual slaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shechita is ritual-- ritual slaughtering, which-- for which he studied in Poland. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name? Let's say that for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Naftali Tsvi Kartagena. And when he came to that community, he really made a lot of difference. A mikvah was built, which is a ritual bath for family purity. And he was very dedicated-- meticulous in his observance-- both sides of the families. And he-- he really made a big difference. It was known in Czechoslovakia, later on, that this is one place where there's kashrut and everything you could hope for.

speaker: interviewer
text: What size of a community was it? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The basic Jewish community-- I don't know. There was a temple and there were-- there was a group of secular Jews. But the orthodox community was-- was small-- I would say maybe 60 families. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --I have a sister who's three years older. She-- What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gitta Blumenthal. She lives in New York. And we went to public school. We walked to school. And it was a carefree time for me, anyway. I spent a lot of time in our garden and went on hikes. We were surrounded by mountains. And things were pretty quiet for a while until we became aware of what was going on in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember in the early years about the relationship between the Jewish people and-- and the-- the Czechs?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good. The-- the relationship was excellent. There were problems with milk, for example. My father insisted that we go to the farm and that it be milk-- the milk be put straight into our pot. There was no thing-- no such thing as pasteurizing.

speaker: interviewer
text: For kashrut reasons, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: For kashrut reasons. And there were various other things where we were limited, but we didn't know any different. So that was fine. We walked to school. We had to go to school on Shabbat, which was always a problem. But it-- it could not be changed, as much as we tried. So what I had to do is rush home on Friday, do my homework, and then take my books back to school. And on-- on Shabbos, I sat there and didn't take notes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they allowed for that, the teachers? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They allowed that. But there was another problem. Because we were very careful in what we were doing. And I could not accept any notes from a Jewish student, because no Jew can write for you on Shabbat. So this--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you couldn't take advantage of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were other students who-- who helped me with the notes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you friendly with the-- the gentile children, as well? It wasn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were friendly, yes, with them-- but mostly associated with Jewish children. And it-- there was a lot of fun. There were hills where we went tobogganing and-- and ice skating-- just a normal childhood-- had patent leather shoes for Yom Tov-- and a new dress, of course. And-- and the home was very warm and very, very-- I have very good memories of that. We spent-- the living quarters were different than they are now. You had a-- a live-in kitchen, where all activities took place. It was a large room where there was a stove. And there was also a couch. And my father kept a [NON-ENGLISH] in a corner, in a-- in a huge cupboard. And all activities took place there. You did your homework in the kitchen, and you sat around there in the evening. And that is-- it was on the main floor. And there was-- there was no refrigerator. You had ice delivered once a week. And that's how you kept your-- you kept your food from spoiling.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't mention your mother yet. What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berta. Her name was a German name. So her name is-- was [NON-ENGLISH]. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned she had started her textile business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she-- when it was decided to-- to go to Reichenberg-- and she was very capable. She was a businesswoman. And her relatives invited her to join and be active in it. She went ahead, actually, and-- while my father studied for cheder. And we stayed with our grandmother. I don't remember her. I was too young. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the year that your family moved to--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1929. And my mother went ahead a year, a year and a 1/2 earlier. So-- and we followed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have memories of when things changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have the-- it was in-- I just started going to school, I think, when people used to come from Germany and tell stories of their businesses being-- going downhill, because gentiles wouldn't shop there anymore. And later on, I remember a man-- and my parents tried to protect my childhood and always sent me out of the room. But I did overhear certain things-- when a man said-- talked about mass suicides. And don't forget, in Germany, people were very patriotic. They had fought in the first war, and they were just totally crushed that there was persecution. And in Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're speaking of the Jews who had--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Poland, you expected that to happen. There was always anti-Semitism, and at the least, provocation. My parents remembered where people would beat up Jews and destroy their property. They handled that, in a way, much better than the German Jews did. And we heard these stories, and we shrugged it off. We said, well, that's Germany. We are living in a democracy where this is not possible. But then things got worse, and as, you know, politically, it looked like they were going to give-- appease Hitler and give him the Sudeten. And at that point, we decided we had to leave. And we packed up our--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1938. And we moved to Prague, which-- it was a big change for us. It-- it meant I was going to a German school at first. Because in the Sudeten, the German schools were better than the Czech. The Czechs were a minority. They had few schools. And that was the reason. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I ask you-- had you noticed a change in the people where you were living? Was there any difference there among--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end, before we left, I did notice some classmates who would meet me on the street and get off the sidewalk. Or I remember one particular boy who lived nearby. And we used to play together and walk to school together. And he-- he wouldn't talk to me. He didn't look at me. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you understand at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 10 or 11. And I knew why. My parents explained to me why. But I just-- I just couldn't-- I couldn't accept it right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made it difficult for him. I said, Walter, don't you see me? Don't you know me? And he turned crimson and walked-- walked on. Those was a-- maybe he had joined the Hitler Youth or whatever. So arriving in Prague was a-- a big change. It was-- Prague is a beautiful city, but it took us awhile to adjust. We lived in a furnished apartment until we found a proper apartment. And my parents went back once more and picked up some belongings-- fruits and things that you prepared for winter, from the garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was just a matter of bringing a little bit of more-- of home into the-- into the new home. And there were blackouts already. And it was-- it was very tense, but there was no trace of anti-Semitism. The Czechs were as-- as burdened and as fearful as we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were convinced now. This is it-- Prague. What would-- would he do in Prague? He's not wanted. The population doesn't want him. The government is against it. It's-- it's a democracy, this is. So I learned a new language. I learned Czech. Went to Czech school. It didn't take very long, because I knew a little bit of it-- and continued in a-- in a Jewish school. There was a Jewish day school of just a few grades. And we-- that's where I went. And-- Do you remember the name of it by any chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. But it was in the Jewish quarter where we lived. And the names in Prague had-- the-- the parts of the-- the city had names. And this was Yosef, of which is Joseph's quarter. And what's interesting is that the-- the original, the-- the old time Czech Jews were named after these quarters. So we had friends whose name was Lieben. They were a famous family, and they came from that district. But we lived in the Jewish district where the Altneuschul is, and there is a-- the old Jewish cemetery. I was fascinated by the history of Prague and really loved the city. And that was going to be my home. But things were getting worse. And the Jewish school was closed down. And we, as--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was by the Czech government who caused the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this-- I'm talking about the time when-- when things were getting bad, when we knew that a war was coming. And things--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the school kind of closed itself down?

speaker: interviewee
text: School sort of closed itself down, or they shrank the school. They-- they had less grades. And we were trying to-- to get out. We-- we lived in a very comfortable apartment. And we tried to leave, but it wasn't enough to have enough money to show the proof that you can pay for your trip. You had to have money outside, in a bank. In order to get an affidavit from-- from Britain, you had to have money in the bank there. We tried Costa Rica. We lined up at the various consulates, but we couldn't get out. We just didn't have the connections.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, we stayed on. And briefly, my parents tried to get us into a children's transfer to go to England. And our relatives sent their children. And we went to the railroad station. And we cried, because parents were separated from their children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the reports started coming in that these were-- there were not always good results. The-- some of these children were misused as maids and weren't sent to school. And some were not placed in orthodox homes, which was promised. And some were bounced from one home to another. And the-- the children wrote all this. And my parents decided against it. They were going to take their chances-- whatever happens. And then, as you know, in 1939, the war broke out. And the-- what they called the Juden [NON-ENGLISH], which drew the various laws for the Jews, came into force. They were posted at the community center. It was really very easy for them to find all the Jews if we-- if we had tried to hide, because there was a community center to which you paid taxes for all the Jewish institutions in-- in the city. And you were registered there. They knew your name, your address, how many in your family, and so on. And I remember that the first-- one of the first Jewish laws that were introduced was to wear the yellow star. And we could purchase those stars. And we had to have it on our garment when we left the house. And this is-- this was a Jewish area. And we-- we had a curfew. We had to be home by 8 o'clock. And we were children. And we played these games. When the old clock in the-- in the other shul started ringing 8:00, that's when we started running home. And I remember, I was stopped by a parade once, and came just two minutes later. And my-- my father was standing in the doorway-- pale. And he really talked to me very, very seriously.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, it was much better if you would beat me up. Why do you say these terrible things to me? Because he explained to me that I was a child. And that if they would catch me, and come home, it would be him who they would arrest, and that I wouldn't want that. And this really shook me up terribly. I was-- I became very careful and never did this again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, things became progressively worse. Our-- the Czech population did not change. Our superintendent used to send me back when I forgot to wear my star. She'd catch me there. And she brought certain items, food items, that we couldn't buy anymore. Places we couldn't go to, she brought to us. We were not allowed to have help in the house. So I remember my father saying he didn't want my mother to scrub the kitchen floor, that we children had to learn how to do that. And we had-- used to have a woman that came in and helped with the laundry. She didn't come anymore. The farmers-- there was one place where we went. My mother was very nutrition conscious. She was way ahead of her time. We'd go and buy fresh vegetables and fruits. And she-- maybe it was because of that that we were well nourished and that we didn't suffer any malnutrition, really-- any signs of it. And they'd buy eggs at-- at ridiculous prices, and always have something special for Shabbos, no matter what it was. She became very inventive, and-- and imitating the former food by substitutes in it. It-- we went on, and it was not-- we adjusted. And my father had a daily [NON-ENGLISH], where he had a chavrusa, where he learned every day. And it was a neighbor. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just say it with a-- a learning partner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And it-- we-- we were-- my sister had to stop her schooling. She was ahead of me, of course. She's older. And my parents enrolled her in a-- in a language school. She studied Spanish and English. And we also used our time. There our parents arranged for someone to come in and teach us how to make artificial flowers out of leather, and all kinds of accessories. And mother said, it's very important to know what to do when you need to earn for your bread. And even if you never know it-- need it, so you would still have it. And we also prepared things that my parents learned from the first world, which didn't apply at all to the second one. They sewed coins and certain valuables into our lapels, and said to watch for that. And if the war breaks out and we have to leave, always remember that you have reserves. And if you're very hungry or if you can save your life with it, use it. And that, apparently, is what you could do in the-- during the world-- first world world war where it-- it didn't apply in our case.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and where are we now? At-- yes. The-- things became worse. There was a German presence in the city. And the Czechs walked. They demonstrated, and cried. They did not wear their uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Hitler Youth uniforms were not seen at first, at all. Later on, there was a sprinkling of it. They refused to join. And many were arrested and persecuted. There was a-- a was very tense time. And then the ghetto came into focus-- Theresienstadt, which was a-- where they kept group-- groups of the military, which housed about 8,000 people. And that's where the transports started going. Prior to that, I had joined the Bnei Akiva. We were organized to help the older people who had to go into transports, to help them with their belongings, and to advise them of what not to try to take at all, and what to leave behind, and what they could carry. And-- and that was very sobering, because you learned so much ahead of time what was coming. I-- I knew what had-- we heard all kind of reports. I knew what it was like, roughly, there. And we knew one of these days they were going to call us. And they did. My father, his chavrusa, his learning partner, when we were called, decided that rather than take a chance of the Nazis cutting his beard in their own way-- that was a very emotional moment. They went to the bathroom and he asked him to shave his beard off. And he came out. He looked entirely different. [CRYING] [DRINKING] [CRYING] Theresienstadt was a-- was an hour away by-- by train. And when I arrived there, I had friends waiting for me there. They knew we were coming. They had lists of who's coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family went together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We always stayed together. And we-- we arrived there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it written down. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I-- it's so hard to keep these dates. In fall '42 we-- we arrived in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told us it's not so bad, that this one is still here. And this one is there. And it's administered by Jews. The-- the-- the Nazis are in the background. And before we know it, we'll be back. We're only an hour away. And that's how they greeted us, and gave us courage, and waited for us there. And in the ghetto, the-- the women were separate from the men, of course. And there were these big rooms with people. I don't know how many women were in one room. You got to know each other. They were from all parts of-- of Czechoslovakia. They were all Czech. And we managed to be in a corner-- us three women-- my sister and my mother. And we arranged a-- a box on Friday. And my father made kiddush. And we had-- when he kept on struggling-- I had a lot of discussions with my father. I wanted to know how come he was there, and everybody else who may have done all kinds of things. We were all together in this one pot. And he said, yes, that is the way it is. When a [NON-ENGLISH] sweeps the world, that-- and-- there are reasons for it. There's no exception made. The-- the honest, and the others, and the good people and those not so good, they're all together. And we are responsible-- one for the other. And he didn't lose his faith. He-- he had a-- you know, reasoned everything out and gave us a lot of courage. And one of the things he had to-- to do-- everybody had to do some work there, which was good. I found out later on, in-- in the concentration camps, that the worst way to exist is not to have anything to do at all, and just watch it happen. And I remember, I saw him. He had a mop, and he was supposed to clean-- it was either the latrine or the-- I don't know what he was cleaning. And I-- and I was very upset about that. And he said, this is the best job around. You do your job well, and you do it quickly. And then the rest of the day, I can learn. He still had his books, and he had his tefillin at that time still. And that filled his day, and was-- he didn't complain. Food was not bad compared to later on. [LAUGHS] And it was-- there was a-- a Jews' elder. We had ghetto money. We had a group of fantastically able and talented people-- musicians and all kind of people. And we started putting on plays. And there were painters there who drew pictures well. There's some of the Theresienstadt artists preserved. And-- but then after a little while, I might mention that in that place of 8-- that held 8,000 people before the war, when we were there, there were about 60,000 Jews crammed into this place. But we had our own doctors and our own people around who-- who took orders from the Nazis. And-- but they were invisible, and it was a self-- sort of self-administered existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you realize that they were using you as kind of a different-- as a show-- place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that-- that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that was not a show place.

speaker: interviewer
text: That wasn't the show place?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the regular Theresienstadt. And people that were officials there had a right to-- to put you on a [NON-ENGLISH]. That means a protective list-- as-- you were their relatives. And there was a-- a man by the name of the Jakob Edelstein, who was a-- a great Zionist and who did tremendous work. He even had the opportunity to get out of Theresienstadt. The Jewish agency offered him escape. And he said, a captain does not leave his ship. And he stayed behind. He had a-- an only child, Ariah, and his wife, and his mother-in-law. And he said to us he was going to protect us as long as he's there. And we didn't know why. Because we really had very little contact with him. And he said the-- this [NON-ENGLISH]. It's the respect of-- that he had for my father. And when we were called to go, the-- we were not on his list yet, somehow. We were called to go, and-- and my mother told Jakob Edelstein that we are going on the transport-- that we are called. We had received our slip. And he said, no, that has to be corrected. And he came to tell us. So my father said the children should stay behind. But he wants to-- he's not going to let another Jew take his place, because each day was important. Maybe next week it will be over and--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry, I have to stop you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for a moment now.
